The last stages of cosmic reionization remain a subject
5
of intense study, with particular interest in whether sub-
1
1 stantial reservoirs of neutral hydrogen persist at redshifts
. below 6. Over the last decade, several lines of evidence
4
have been put forward to support the hypothesis of these
0  “  ”
so-called  neutral islands,  each offering interesting but
5
indirect probes into the ionization state of the intergalac-
2
: tic medium (IGM). The first such argument was based on
v the observed distribution of mean opacities in the spectra
i
of high redshift quasars (Bosman et al. 2018; Eilers et al.
X 2018; Kulkarni et al. 2019; Keating et al. 2020; Nasir &
r
D’Aloisio 2020; Bosman et al. 2022) and another relies
a
on the observed abundance of long gaps in the spectra
(Becker et al. 2015; Zhu et al. 2021). Unfortunately, both
of these arguments are model-dependent, and there ex-
ist counterexamples to both of them (Yang et al. 2020;
Gnedin 2022; Garaldi et al. 2022).
The challenge of using indirect probes of the ioniza-
tion state of the IGM at z > 5 is illustrated in Figure
1.  It shows a synthetic spectrum at z = 5.5 from one
of the simulations described below. The original spec-
trum, shown in blue, exhibits well-known transmission
spikes.  In black, we show a toy model that introduces
a large “neutral island” by manually setting the hydro-

−3
fraction inside the “island” is set to 10  instead of 1.
Since the optical depth of the neutral IGM at the mean
5
density at this redshift is around 6 × 10 , even in deep
voids (with overdensity of a few percent), a neutral frac-
−3
tion of 10  produces complete absorption (τLyα ∼ 10).
−3
The only key difference between xHI = 10  and xHI = 1
is the suppression of flux outside of a neutral region com-
−3
pared to the xHI = 10  case, visible as the difference
between black and purple (the overlay of solid blue and
translucent red) lines between ∼ 5/h and 15/h cMpc and
between 35/h and ∼ 45/h cMpc. This suppression arises
from the extended damping wing of the Lyα absorption
cross-section, which has a Lorentzian profile with broad
−2
wings that fall off as (ν − ν0)  , where ν is the pho-
ton frequency and ν0  is the Lyα resonance frequency.
As a result, even regions of the IGM that lie outside a
neutral patch and are themselves highly ionized can still
experience significant absorption due to the proximity
of neutral hydrogen. Because the damping wing optical
depth is directly proportional to the total column den-
sity of neutral hydrogen, it differs substantially between
−3
xHI = 10  and xHI = 1.  In the fully neutral case,
the higher neutral hydrogen abundance leads to a strong

the “neutral island” due to the damping wings.
damping wing, which absorbs photons over a broader fre-
quency (or spatial) range beyond the edges of the region.
In contrast, a lower neutral fraction produces no damp-
ing wings and thus minimal absorption outside the core
of the patch.
As was first pointed out by Malloy & Lidz (2015),
detecting such flux suppression is possibly the only ro-
bust signature of a genuine mostly neutral region in the
IGM. Recently, three different papers reported the detec-
tion of the “Malloy-Lidz” effect in the observational data
(Becker et al. 2024; Spina et al. 2024; Zhu et al. 2024a).
There are two main observational challenges with such
detection.

First, the spectra are noisy, and transmission
spikes are rare, so it is difficult to detect the damping
wing effect in a single spectrum (but see Becker et al.
2024) and hence stacking several spectra can be benefi-
cial (Spina et al. 2024; Zhu et al. 2024a). The second
challenge is that the actual locations of neutral islands
are not known,  so from the Lyα absorption spectrum
alone, the edge of the damping wing cannot be deter-
mined. Becker et al. (2024), Spina et al. (2024), and Zhu
et al. (2024a) solved the latter problem by cleverly using
the Lyβ absorption spectra to determine where the gaps
end. In all three papers, the detection has been claimed,
with perhaps the most easily interpretable example pro-
vided by Figure 1 of Zhu et al. (2024a).
There are at least two potential pitfalls in interpret-
ing these detections. First, Lyβ gaps are expected to be
narrower than Lyα gaps. This is because transmission
spikes predominantly arise in deep voids (Garaldi et al.
2019; Zhu et al. 2024b), and since Lyβ has a lower ab-
sorption cross-section,  it is more likely to show a spike
where Lyα remains fully absorbed. As a result, observers

overlay of solid blue and translucent red) lines, immediately outside
using Lyβ transmission spikes to define the ends of gaps
may inadvertently align the Lyα spectra too early - that
is, while Lyα is still within the dark part of the gap.
This can artificially create a reduction in Lyα flux near
the nominal edge, mimicking a damping wing even in
the absence of a neutral region (Zhu et al. 2024a). The
second pitfall goes in the opposite direction:  if the Lyβ
transmission spike that ends a gap also appears in Lyα,
then stacking Lyα spectra at that position will reveal a
flux spike, not a suppression. This could mask the pres-
ence of a genuine damping wing or lead to a mistaken
conclusion that the IGM is fully ionized.  In both cases,
careful interpretation is required, as the behavior of Lyβ
and Lyα transmission spikes is not always one-to-one,
and incorrect alignment can either mimic or obscure the
subtle signal of a damping wing.
The original goal of this short paper was to explore
whether these potential pitfalls subvert the observational
detections and to check whether the damping-wing-like
feature at the edge of a gap is indeed associated with
“neutral islands”. For that task, the locations of “neu-
tral  islands” must be known, so this can only be done
in numerical simulations of cosmic reionization. The two
simulation sets available to us are the “Cosmic Reion-
ization On Computers” (CROC, Gnedin 2014; Gnedin
& Kaurov 2014; Gnedin 2022) and “Thesan” (Kannan
et al. 2022; Garaldi et al. 2022, 2024). The two simula-
tions are very similar in many respects: they have similar
volumes (∼ 100 cMpc) and spatial resolutions (100-300
3
proper pc), almost identical mass resolution (2000 ), and
model a similar range of relevant physical effects: gas dy-
namics, non-equilibrium cooling and ionization, radiative
transfer, and star formation and stellar feedback. They.

Producing synthetic Lyβ spectra from simulations is
non-trivial.  While synthetic Lyα spectra for a nar-
row redshift interval can be easily produced from the
simulation data, the Lyβ forest overlaps with the Lyα
forest at a lower redshift.  High-resolution simulation
boxes are not  large enough to cover the full  spectral
range from Lyα to Lyβ. Hence, at least two indepen-
dent snapshots must be used. For the case in question,
Zhu et al. (2024a) identified gaps at the Lyα transition
wavelength at z ∼ 5.9. The Lyβ transition at this red-
shift has the same wavelength as the Lyα transition at
z = 1026˚A/1216˚A × (1 + 5.9) − 1 = 4.8. CROC sim-
ulations barely reach that cosmic time (being stopped
at z = 5), and the largest Thesan simulation, Thesan-1,
is stopped even earlier.  In addition, different synthetic
spectra are usually generated along random lines of sight
at each simulation snapshot, but the random directions
and starting points are often the same for all snapshots
(this is the case for both CROC and Thesan). Hence,
care must be taken to ensure that the Lyα forest at
z = 5.9 and z = 4.8 are not artificially correlated by
accidentally using identically oriented sightlines.
This is easy to achieve for CROC since it includes sev-
eral simulations starting with independent realizations of
cosmological initial conditions. Here we use the simula-
tion box labeled “C80C.DC=-1” for z = 5.9 Lyα and
z = 5 Lyβ forests and another run, “C80A”,  for the
z = 5 Lyα forests.  The “C80C.DC=-1” box matches
the observational constraints on the abundance of dark
gaps (Gnedin 2022) and hence is the most suitable of all
CROC boxes for modeling dark gaps. For Thesan-1, we
select two different lines of sight for the z = 5.9 and z = 5
Lyα forest.  In addition, the publicly released Thesan-1
data do not actually include synthetic quasar spectra at

z = 5.  Instead, we use the latest available snapshot at
z = 5.5 and rescale the Lyα forest at that snapshot to
z = 4.8 using the average evolution of the Lyα opacity
from Yang et al. (2020).
We then follow the Zhu et al. (2024a) procedure to
generate synthetic observations:  identify Lyα and Lyβ
gaps in the spectra as continuous regions with the flux
below the thresholds of 0.05 and 0.02, align the Lyβ gaps
at their left and right edges, and compute the average
Lyα flux outside the gap edge as a function of velocity
away from the edge. We use 1000 lines of sight for CROC
and 300 for Thesan (all that is available).
Results of this exercise are shown in Figure 2. Neither
CROC nor Thesan shows the damping-wing-like feature
despite having the number of dark gaps per unit length
comparable to the observed frequency, and this result re-
mains robust irrespective of whether observational noise
is added to the synthetic spectra as long as the noise
level does not exceed the actual noise in observational
measurements.  In the CROC “C80C.DC=-1” simula-
tion, reionization ends too early at z = 6.7 and thus no
“neutral islands” remain at z = 5.9. In Thesan, reioniza-
tion proceeds more gradually and is not finished at the
end point of the simulation at z = 5.5. Neutral gas at

around the mean cosmic densities still exists in Thesan-
1 at z = 5.9, but apparently,  there is not enough of
that gas left to produce the damping-wing-like features
around the dark gaps. Figure 3 shows the most impor-
tant properties - the mean neutral fraction and the mean
density - of dark gaps in the two simulations, where the
averaging is done over each gap length.

Both simulations
have large numbers of dark gaps that are not neutral is-
lands.
In order to explore the discrepancies between simu-
lations and observations better, we manually add 20/h
cMpc neutral  islands to each CROC sightline (at this
size, the actual size of the island becomes irrelevant, the
damping wings from islands larger than 20/h cMpc do
not depend on the island size), as is illustrated in Figure
4, and repeat the procedure for stacking the dark gaps.
With 1000 sightlines, this adds 1000 neutral  islands to
1500 dark gaps already present in the original simulation
data (i.e. with neutral  islands making 40% of all dark
gaps). Figure 5 shows the stacked dark gap profiles for
this test case - the 40% neutral  island fraction is not
enough to exhibit any sign of the damping wing.  In or-
der to increase that fraction, we lower the thresholds for
the dark gap detection (0.05 for Lyα and 0.02 for Lyβ)
uniformly by factors of 3, 10, and 30, resulting in the
neutral island fraction increasing to 75%, 90%, and 96%,

manually. Different lines show tests with progressively larger frac-
tions of neutral islands among all gaps (achieved by lowering the
thresholds for gap detection since neutral islands have effectively
zero flux). Only when the fraction of neutral islands exceeds 90%
does the damping wing feature become unambiguously detected.

tral  islands is effectively zero. As Figure 5 shows, the
damping wing feature only becomes unambiguously de-.

The conclusions of this paper would be simple - the
simulations fail to reproduce the detection of the damp-
ing wing feature in observational data - if one nagging
question was not still  lingering:  where are the “non-
island” dark gaps in the observational data? These are
abundant in both CROC and Thesan.  In fact, all dark
gaps in CROC simulations are “non-island”, and their
abundance is consistent with observations. Given that
the damping wing feature is only observable when neu-
tral  islands make more than 90% of all dark gaps, the
absence of these “non-island” gaps like the second one in
Figure 4 in observations is extremely puzzling.  In fact,
this was already noticed by Zhu et al. (2024a, see their
Fig. 4e).
Another surprising feature of the observational claims
is the long persistence of neutral islands. An ionization
front propagates into a neutral island with the mean neu-
tral hydrogen density nH with the velocity vI that satis-
fies simple photon conservation:
nHvI = cnI ,
where nI is the incident number density of ionizing pho-
tons. The left-hand side is the rate (per unit area) at
which neutral hydrogen atoms are ionized by the advanc-
ing front, and the right-hand side is the flux of ionizing
photons. The latter is easy to estimate from the obser-
vationally determined photoionization rate Γ,
Γ = cσInI ,
where σI is the effective cross-section for an ionizing pho-

5  −2 −1
value, cnI ≈ 1.5 × 10 cm  s  and vI ≈ 24,000 km/s.
At this speed, a 10 cMpc neutral  island (a value used
by Zhu et al. (2021)) is ionized in 60 Myr, and probably
even faster, since such a long sightline is highly likely to
cross a cosmic void and thus be on average underdense,
plus Γ at z = 5.6 is twice as large as at z = 5.9. Hence,
it is rather surprising that Spina et al. (2024) detected
the damping wing features as late as z = 5.6 (and for
significantly shorter gaps).
In summary,  it appears that the observational detec-
tions of damping wing features present serious challenges
both to state-of-the-art simulations and even to our gen-
eral understanding of how reionization proceeds.
We thank ChatGPT for producing most of the anal-
ysis code used in this work and the Thesan team for
making their data publicly available. The earlier draft
of  this manuscript was substantially improved by de-
tailed comments from Yongda Zhu and George Becker.
